meta.title: Ocean Quiz
meta.author: Penny Atcheson and Elizabeth Fox
meta.focusQuestion: How does teamwork and individual preparation affect the outcome of a competition?

sections.title: Chapter 1
paragraphs: Just as Maggie and her parents sat down for dinner, the telephone rang. Maggie sprang up to answer it, dashing into the other room and leaving a clattering fork on the floor behind her. She shouted so loudly that her parents heard every word.
"Did we make it?" she gasped, without even saying hello. "You're kidding; who else? Oh no, not that know-it-all. Well whatever, as long as you and I are on the team. I'll see you tomorrow."

Maggie hopped back into the kitchen, where her parents were smiling. "Mom, Dad, we made it! Caroline and I are going to represent the school at the Kid Quiz Bowl, and we might even win a school field day at Shamu Ocean World!"
"Slow down, Maggie, you haven't won yet," Maggie's mother laughed. "I'll bet it's going to be a lot of responsibility representing the entire school."
"And you shouldn't get so wrapped up in the contest that you neglect your other subjects," said her father.

Maggie giggled, "I'll be fine, and besides, we'll be learning as we practice for the Quiz Bowl. The topic this year is oceans, which is pretty much the whole planet, since two-thirds of the earth is covered with water."
Before her parents could open their mouths, Maggie bounded up the stairs, still hollering about winning the Bowl, going to Shamu Ocean World, swimming with dolphins, and kissing whales.

sections.title: Chapter 2
paragraphs: The next day, Maggie and her team got out of math and met in the school library. Maggie and Caroline were the second ones there, and sitting at a table was Tad, leaning over a book.
Tad looked up. "The first order of business, I think, is for us to decide on a name. A team name is critical for defining who we are and intimidating our competitors," he said.
Just then, Bert rushed in, a little out of breath. "Sorry I'm late," he said, blushing.
"I nominate Panthalassa, which was the first giant ocean that existed before the continental plates moved and divided," Tad said.

"Theoretically, you could sail from one ocean to all the others without ever touching land," Tad explained. "We simply divide the oceans for our convenience, but all the continents are just massive islands in an even larger ocean."

"Okay, okay," Maggie interrupted, "there's too much information to cover for us to waste time worrying about our name. I've put the names of the different zones of the ocean floor in my bag. We'll each choose one, research it, and then share what we've found."
"Continental shelf," Maggie read. "Ah, yes," Tad sniffed, "you'll be looking into the shallowest part of the ocean where the land first submerges."
"Continental slope," Caroline read. "The slope is the cliff-like boundary between the continental shelf and the deep ocean floor," Tad said.

"And now for my topic... the abyssal plain. I'll be covering the entire ocean floor, including mountain ranges, valleys, hills, faults, and even seamounts, which are undersea volcanic mountains higher than the Himalayas."

Bert reached in and drew the last piece of paper. "Ocean trenches," he said. "Bert, I envy you; you'll be studying the most recent discoveries about the deepest and darkest places in the ocean. Trade with me," said Tad.
"No trades," Maggie insisted. "We don't have time. There are only three weeks until the Kid Quiz Bowl."

sections.title: Chapter 3
paragraphs: Caroline flopped down in a chair in front of a library computer. She logged on to the Internet and typed "continental slope" into a search engine. She started searching for Web sites about movie stars, then saw Tad browsing the shelves and quickly went back to oceans.

The continental slope was like an underwater cliff, sometimes dropping from 200 meters (660 ft) down to 5,000 meters (3 mi). Rivers deposited sediment onto the shelf until the sediment pushed to the edge of the slope and rained down the cliff wall.

Scientists launched the submarine in the water above the continental slope. All along the slope grew sun-loving ocean plants and animals. She spotted brain coral and sea pens, sea urchins, shrimp and lobster, even an octopus.

Then the water began to get darker, and the plants began to disappear, leaving just squid and some dark-colored fish. When a tiny glowing animal swam into the light, it turned out to be totally transparent.
With a whoosh, the submarine brushed the bottom, churning up clouds of dust. This was the muddy sediment that sloughed off from the river deposits on the continental shelf.

Bert couldn't seem to find any information on ocean trenches. The librarian told him that most of the research was pretty new, so it wasn't widely available. He finally found something in the Guinness Book of World Records.
The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean was the deepest point on Earth, at 9.6 kilometers (6 mi) deep. In 1960, Swiss scientist Jacques Piccard dove down into the trench in his bathyscaphe.

Maggie tried the same approach, typing "continental shelf" into a search engine. But instead of giving up when she got hundreds of hits, she began to poke around. She found out that the continental shelf received the most sunlight, which allowed more sea life to function there than anywhere else.

Tad walked up one library aisle and down another. His books at home had plenty of information about the abyssal plain. He knew that the plain covered almost 75 percent of the ocean floor. The total area covered 200 million square kilometers (77 million sq mi).
He also knew that the creatures in the abyssal plain were completely freaky, like the giant squid. This research is a snap, he thought, without ever opening a book.
A few days before the Quiz Bowl, the kids came together and shared their information. "This is awesome, guys. You brought back tons of facts," Maggie said.
"Well, except you, Bert," Tad said. "Couldn't you find anything else to report on besides the deepest trench?"
"Well," Bert stammered, "I sort of ran out of time. It's tough with all my other classes, and you wouldn't believe how little information there was."
"But what about the undersea geysers? The mineral-spewing chimneys that send boiling toxic plumes into the ocean depths?" Tad demanded.
"Okay, it's all right," Maggie said. "We have plenty to work with. We all just have to work as a team, because the Quiz Bowl is this weekend."

sections.title: Chapter 4
paragraphs: "I'm so nervous I can't see," Caroline whispered to Maggie, peeking out from behind the black curtain onto the stage. The bright stage lights shone right in their eyes.
Bert nervously chewed a pencil, Caroline hopped from foot to foot, and Maggie could feel her palms sweating. The only one who looked calm was Tad.

"Welcome, everyone, to the final round of the seventh annual Kid Quiz Bowl competition. We're down to our last two teams, and the one that wins this round will receive the free trip to Shamu Ocean World!"
The teams went back and forth, one breaking into the lead, the other catching up, until all thirty questions had been asked. It was fifteen and fifteen—a tie, so there had to be a tiebreaker.
"Since this question will break the tie and determine the overall winner, each team may nominate one member to represent it."
"Why haven't you been answering any questions, Tad?" asked Maggie. "Oh, these questions have all been pretty simple, so I thought I'd give the rest of you guys a chance," Tad said.
The team agreed to send Tad out. "He'd better be as smart as he acts," Caroline muttered.
"Here is the tie-breaking question: What is the name of the deepest trench in the ocean, and in which ocean is it located?"
Maggie started laughing, Caroline jumped up and down, and Bert did a victory dance. There was no way Tad could miss this one.
Buzz! "Deerfield?" "All right!" Bert burst out. Then, everything seemed quiet for an awfully long time.
"Fifteen seconds, Deerfield." Tad stared blankly ahead. "I'm sorry, Deerfield, but your time is up. Shelbyville, your question."
"The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean," the Shelbyville student answered confidently. "You are correct! Shelbyville School is going to spend a field day at Shamu Ocean World!"

sections.title: Chapter 5
paragraphs: Back at school, Maggie, Caroline, Tad, and Bert met at their table in the library.
"What happened to you out there, Tad?" scolded Caroline. "You didn't even blink, and we depended on your brains to win."
"Yeah, even I knew the answer to the trench question," Bert said.

Tad stared down at his hands and muttered something. "I just said I'm sorry I let you guys down. I froze, I choked, I blew it. I knew the answer, but I kept remembering how I treated Bert, and how ironic it was that his information ended up being what we really needed."
"I guess it could have happened to any of us," Maggie said. "Oh well, we made a good show, and after all, we were tied until the very end."
"Hey," Caroline said, "I hear that next year's topic might be the solar system, and the prize will be a trip to the planetarium."

"Well that's a cakewalk!" Tad cried. "I must already know everything there is to know about the solar system, the moons and the asteroid belt and gravitational arcs...." Tad stopped and began backing away from the glares of his teammates. Then he turned and ran, with Caroline, Bert, and Maggie chasing him, laughing.

vocabulary.definition: the ocean zone that covers the deep ocean floor; a bottomless depth
vocabulary.sentence: And now for my topic... the abyssal plain.

vocabulary.definition: a small submarine used for extremely deep diving
vocabulary.sentence: In 1960, Swiss scientist Jacques Piccard dove down into the trench in his bathyscaphe.
A team name is critical for defining who we are and intimidating our competitors.

vocabulary.definition: the opposite of what is expected; a twist where something turns out opposite of what it seems
vocabulary.sentence: I kept remembering how I treated Bert, and how ironic it was that his information ended up being what we really needed.
The mineral-spewing chimneys that send boiling toxic plumes into the ocean depths.

vocabulary.definition: a stand with a microphone for public speaking
vocabulary.sentence: The host stood at a podium with a booming microphone.
Even seamounts, which are undersea volcanic mountains higher than the Himalayas.

vocabulary.definition: sand, dirt, and soil in water
vocabulary.sentence: Rivers deposited sediment onto the shelf until the sediment pushed to the edge of the slope.
You'll be looking into the shallowest part of the ocean where the land first submerges.
Maggie, Caroline, and Bert stood sullenly in the wings.
The little glowing animal turned out to be totally transparent.

vocabulary.definition: according to assumed facts; in theory
vocabulary.sentence: Theoretically, you could sail from one ocean to all the others without ever touching land.

vocabulary.definition: cracks in the sea floor; deep valleys in the ocean
vocabulary.sentence: Bert reached in and drew the last piece of paper. "Ocean trenches," he said.

questions.question: What is the main problem of this story?
questions.evidence: Maggie, Caroline, Tad, and Bert want to win the Kid Quiz Bowl. They research ocean topics and compete against Shelbyville.
choices: Maggie, Caroline, Tad, and Bert want to win the Quiz Bowl contest.
Maggie and Caroline don't want to be on a team with Tad.
The team wants to study hard and pass all their classes.
Tad wants to make sure everyone knows how much information he has learned.
questions.explanation: The main problem is that the team wants to win the Quiz Bowl contest and must work together to prepare.

questions.question: If something submerges, what happens?
questions.evidence: You'll be looking into the shallowest part of the ocean where the land first submerges.
questions.explanation: To submerge means to sink under the water.

questions.question: Why did Bert have trouble finding information on ocean trenches?
questions.evidence: The librarian told him that most of the research was pretty new, so it wasn't widely available.
choices: No one knows anything about these.
He didn't take the time to do research.
The information is new and not in many books.
The information is too old to be worthwhile.
questions.explanation: Research on ocean trenches is new and not widely available in books yet.

questions.question: Why was Maggie looking forward to the Kid Quiz Bowl?
questions.evidence: Maggie and Caroline are going to represent the school. They might win a school field day at Shamu Ocean World. The team members get out of class for an hour a day to get ready.
choices: She and Caroline were on a team together.
They could win a field trip for the whole school.
She would get out of class every day to get ready.
questions.explanation: All three reasons are true — she was excited about being on a team with Caroline, winning a trip, and getting out of class to prepare.

questions.question: What words best describe Bert?
questions.evidence: Bert rushed in late, blushing. He couldn't seem to find any information on trenches. He told himself he would only work on his history report for ten minutes, but when he looked up, it was an hour later.
questions.explanation: Bert is unfocused (distracted by his history report) and disorganized (couldn't find information in time).

questions.question: How could you tell Maggie's team did well at the competition?
questions.evidence: It was fifteen and fifteen—a tie. They made it to the final round of the competition.
choices: They had made it to the final round of the competition.
They had done their research and knew the facts.
questions.explanation: The best evidence that they did well is that they made it to the final round, where they tied 15-15 with Shelbyville.

questions.question: Comparing the two teams in the finals, you can tell:
questions.evidence: It was fifteen and fifteen—a tie, so there had to be a tiebreaker. The teams went back and forth, one breaking into the lead, the other catching up.
choices: None of the students had studied.
questions.explanation: The 15-15 tie shows that both teams were equally matched in knowledge and speed.

questions.question: What is sediment?
questions.evidence: Rivers deposited sediment onto the shelf. This was the muddy sediment that sloughed off from the river deposits.
choices: A crack in the floor of the sea.
A type of rock found in oceans.
Dirt and mud mixed in water.
questions.explanation: Sediment is sand, dirt, and soil in water — deposited by rivers onto the continental shelf.

questions.question: Why did Tad not answer the last question?
questions.evidence: Tad stared blankly ahead. I froze, I choked, I blew it. I knew the answer, but I kept remembering how I treated Bert, and how ironic it was.
choices: He forgot to push the button.
questions.explanation: Tad froze from pressure and guilt. He knew the answer but couldn't speak because he remembered how he had treated Bert.

questions.question: What is an undersea volcano called?
questions.evidence: Seamounts, which are undersea volcanic mountains higher than the Himalayas.
questions.explanation: A seamount is an undersea volcano — an underwater mountain formed by volcanic activity.

questions.question: At the end of the story, why were Maggie, Caroline, and Bert laughing and chasing Tad? Use evidence from the text.
questions.explanation: Think about how Tad acted during the preparation and competition. What lesson did the story teach?
opinionPrompts: How did Tad treat his teammates during preparation?
What happened when the team needed Tad most?
Why did the team chase Tad at the end — were they angry or playful?

grammar.title: Quotation Marks in Dialogue
grammar.explanation: When characters speak in a story, their words are enclosed in quotation marks. Punctuation marks (commas, periods, question marks, exclamation marks) go inside the closing quotation mark.
rules: Opening quotation mark (") goes before the first spoken word
Comma goes inside the closing quotation mark before the dialogue tag: "Did we make it?" she gasped.
End punctuation (., !, ?) goes inside the closing quotation mark: "We won!" they cheered.
When dialogue is interrupted, each part gets its own quotation marks: "I think," she said, "we should study."

exercises.question: Which sentence uses quotation marks correctly?
exercises.explanation: The question mark goes inside the quotation mark, and no extra comma is needed.

exercises.question: Which sentence has correct dialogue punctuation?
exercises.explanation: The exclamation mark goes inside the quotation mark.

exercises.question: Find the sentence with correct interrupted dialogue:
options: "I think," she said, "we should study more."
"I think" she said, "we should study more."
"I think, she said, we should study more."
"I think" she said "we should study more."
exercises.explanation: When dialogue is interrupted by a tag, each part needs quotation marks and commas.

exercises.sentence: Add the missing quotation marks: Mom, Dad, we made it! Maggie shouted.
options: "Mom, Dad, we made it!" Maggie shouted.
"Mom, Dad, we made it", Maggie shouted.
"Mom, Dad, we made it!", Maggie shouted.
Mom, Dad, "we made it!" Maggie shouted.
exercises.explanation: The entire spoken sentence goes inside quotation marks, with the exclamation mark inside.

exercises.sentence: Choose the correct version: The host said, "Deerfield, I need your answer."
options: The host said, "Deerfield, I need your answer."
The host said "Deerfield, I need your answer".
The host said, Deerfield, "I need your answer."
"The host said, Deerfield, I need your answer."
exercises.explanation: A comma introduces the quoted dialogue, and the period goes inside the quotation marks.

chapter1.title: Kid Quiz Bowl
chapter1.content: A quiz competition between school teams. This year's topic: OCEANS! Maggie is SO excited!

chapter1.title: Character: Maggie
chapter1.content: Maggie springs up, shouts, giggles, bounds up stairs — she's HIGH ENERGY and EXCITED about the quiz!

chapter2.title: Abyssal / Theoretically / Submerges
chapter2.content: ABYSSAL = deep ocean floor. THEORETICALLY = according to assumed facts. SUBMERGES = sinks under water. These are Tad's favorite big words!

chapter2.title: Character: Tad
chapter2.content: Tad 'sniffs' and 'pushes up his glasses' — he's a KNOW-IT-ALL who thinks everyone else's topic is easy!

chapter3.title: Sediment / Transparent / Bathyscaphe / Seamounts
chapter3.content: SEDIMENT = dirt in water. TRANSPARENT = see-through. BATHYSCAPHE = deep-sea submarine. SEAMOUNTS = underwater volcanoes!

chapter3.title: Compare the Researchers
chapter3.content: Maggie: pokes around, follows links → DEEP research. Caroline: watches videos → okay research. Tad: thinks he already knows → NO research. Bert: gets distracted → LITTLE research.

chapter4.title: Podium / Sullenly
chapter4.content: PODIUM = raised platform for speaking. SULLY = sadly and angrily. The team stands SULLY after losing!

chapter4.title: Irony Alert!
chapter4.content: Tad picked the 'easy' abyssal plain topic and mocked Bert's trench topic. But the TIEBREAKER was about TRENCHES! How IRONIC!

chapter5.title: Ironic / Cakewalk
chapter5.content: IRONIC = opposite of what's expected. CAKEWALK = something very easy. Tad thinks the solar system topic is a CAKEWALK... but we know what happened last time!

chapter5.title: Story Resolution
chapter5.content: Tad apologizes — he froze because of GUILT. Maggie forgives him. Next topic: solar system. Tad's about to make the SAME mistake again! The team chases him LAUGHING — they've learned to have fun together.
